columns.extraction_plan: The experimental arm received orteronel (300 mg oral twice daily) in addition to ADT. The control arm received bicalutamide (50 mg oral once daily) in addition to ADT. This information is found in the 'METHODS' section of the abstract on page 1.

plans.group_name: Class of Agent in Treatment Arm 1

columns.column_name: Class of Agent in Treatment Arm 1
columns.extraction_plan: The abstract on page 1 states that the experimental arm received 'ADT with orteronel'. The introduction on page 1 describes Orteronel (TAK-700) as 'a novel CYP17 inhibitor'.

The abstract on page 1 states: 'Among 1,279 patients included in the analysis, 638 were randomly assigned to the ADT plus orteronel arm and 641 to the control arm.' Table 1 on page 5, under 'Bicalutamide (n = 641)', confirms this number. Figure 1 on page 3 and Figure 2 on page 4 also show 'LHRHa plus bicalutamide' with 'At Risk' 641. Figure A1 on page 13 shows the ITT analysis population for the LHRHa plus bicalutamide arm as (n = 641).

The abstract on page 1 states: "Among 1,279 patients included in the analysis, 638 were randomly assigned to the ADT plus orteronel arm and 641 to the control arm." Table 1 on page 5, titled "Baseline Characteristics of Patients Included in the Primary Intention-To-Treat Analysis (N = 1,279)", lists "Bicalutamide (n = 641)" as the control arm. Figure A1, the CONSORT diagram on page 13, shows "ITT analysis population (n = 641) Treatment status" for the LHRHa plus bicalutamide arm. Therefore, the control arm has 641 participants.

Extract the median age '67.6' from the 'Median age, years (range)' row under the 'Orteronel (n = 638)' column in Table 1 on page 5. This is corroborated by text on page 4 stating 'The median age of the patients for the orteronel ... arms was 67.6 ... years, respectively'.

Extract the median age '68.1' from the 'Median age, years (range)' row under the 'Bicalutamide (n = 641)' column in Table 1 on page 5. This is corroborated by text on page 4 stating 'The median age of the patients for the ... control arms was ... 68.1 years, respectively'.

The total number of patients randomly assigned to the trial is 1,313. This is stated in the 'Patients' section on page 4: 'Between March 1, 2013, and July 15, 2017, 1,313 patients were randomly assigned'. Figure A1 on page 13, the CONSORT diagram, also shows 'Total randomly assigned (N = 1,313)'.

The median follow-up duration for the SWOG S1216 trial is reported as 58.8 months in Table A2 (page 12), under the 'Median Follow-Up (months)' column. This is consistent with '4.9 years' mentioned in the abstract on page 1 and in the 'Statistical Analysis' section and Figure 1 on page 3 (4.9 years * 12 months/year = 58.8 months).
Extract the NCT number from the 'CLINICAL TRIAL INFORMATION' section on page 8.
I looked on page 1 for publication details and in the references section on pages 8 and 9, but a PubMed ID (PMID) was not explicitly reported. A DOI is provided on page 1.
Extract the trial name 'SWOG-1216' from the article title on page 1.
Extract the first author's name 'Neeraj Agarwal' from the author list on page 1.
Extract the publication year '2022' from the copyright information at the bottom of page 1.
The document is a 'Full Pub'. This is indicated by the presence of a DOI (page 1), extensive sections like 'METHODS', 'RESULTS', 'DISCUSSION', 'REFERENCES', and 'AUTHORS' DISCLOSURES' (pages 1-11).
Extract 'Phase III' from the article title and abstract on page 1, and confirmed in the 'Trial Design and Conduct' section on page 2.

The title 'A Multicenter, Randomized, Open-Label Phase III Trial (SWOG-1216)' on page 1 indicates this is the original publication of the trial results. The abstract mentions 'After a median follow-up of 4.9 years', which is part of the initial reporting of the trial's outcomes.

Docetaxel was not part of the primary treatment in the experimental (Orteronel + ADT) arm of the SWOG-1216 trial. While docetaxel is mentioned as a subsequent life-prolonging therapy (page 6, Discussion), Table A1 (page 12) provides the total N (%) for 'Receiving life-prolonging treatment' (203 patients, 61.3%) but does not break down this total by specific therapies like docetaxel. Therefore, the N (%) for docetaxel administration in the treatment arm is not reported.

Docetaxel was not part of the primary treatment in the control (Bicalutamide + ADT) arm of the SWOG-1216 trial. While docetaxel is mentioned as a subsequent life-prolonging therapy (page 6, Discussion), Table A1 (page 12) provides the total N (%) for 'Receiving life-prolonging treatment' (311 patients, 77.4%) but does not break down this total by specific therapies like docetaxel. Therefore, the N (%) for docetaxel administration in the control arm is not reported.

The median on-treatment duration for the treatment arm (Orteronel) is not explicitly reported in the PDF. Table A1 (page 12) provides the 'Protocol/Postprotocol Treatment Status' showing the number of patients still on protocol treatment (192 out of 638) and those off protocol treatment (446 out of 638), but it does not provide a median duration for how long patients received the treatment. The abstract (page 1) and results section (page 4) mention a median follow-up of 4.9 years, but this refers to the study follow-up, not the duration of active treatment.

The median on-treatment duration for the control arm (Bicalutamide) is not explicitly reported in the PDF. Table A1 (page 12) provides the 'Protocol/Postprotocol Treatment Status' showing the number of patients still on protocol treatment (100 out of 641) and those off protocol treatment (541 out of 641), but it does not provide a median duration for how long patients received the treatment. The abstract (page 1) and results section (page 4) mention a median follow-up of 4.9 years, but this refers to the study follow-up, not the duration of active treatment.

The 'METHODS' section on page 1 states: 'patients with metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer were randomly assigned 1:1 to ADT with orteronel (300 mg oral twice daily; experimental arm) or ADT with bicalutamide (50 mg oral once daily; control arm).' This indicates two arms. Page 2, under 'Patients and Interventions', further confirms: 'Patients were randomly assigned in a 1:1 ratio to receive orteronel (300 mg) orally twice daily or bicalutamide (50 mg) administered orally once daily, in addition to continuous ADT.' Thus, there are 2 arms.
The median OS for the orteronel (treatment) arm is reported as 81.1 months in the abstract on page 1, confirmed in Figure 1 on page 3, and in the 'Primary End Point' section on page 4.
The median OS for the bicalutamide (control) arm is reported as 70.2 months in the abstract on page 1, confirmed in Figure 1 on page 3, and in the 'Primary End Point' section on page 4.

In Table A3 on page 13, under the 'Extensive' disease group (which corresponds to high volume disease as per footnote 'b' on page 12), the 'OS Orteronel Median/5 Year' is 4.8 years. Converting to months: 4.8 years * 12 months/year = 57.6 months.

In Table A3 on page 13, under the 'Extensive' disease group (which corresponds to high volume disease as per footnote 'b' on page 12), the 'OS Control Median/5 Year' is 4.1 years. Converting to months: 4.1 years * 12 months/year = 49.2 months.

In Table A3 on page 13, under the 'Minimal' disease group (which corresponds to low volume disease as per footnote 'b' on page 12), the 'OS Orteronel Median/5 Year' is reported as 'NR' (Not Reached). Therefore, a specific median OS in months is not available.

In Table A3 on page 13, under the 'Minimal' disease group (which corresponds to low volume disease as per footnote 'b' on page 12), the 'OS Control Median/5 Year' is reported as 'NR' (Not Reached). Therefore, a specific median OS in months is not available.

columns.column_name: Mode of metastases - N (%) | Synchronous | Treatment
columns.extraction_plan: I examined Table 1 (page 5), titled "Baseline Characteristics of Patients Included in the Primary Intention-To-Treat Analysis," which lists various patient characteristics for both the Orteronel (treatment) and Bicalutamide (control) arms. This table includes details such as age, race, Zubrod PS, Gleason score, PSA level, and presence of metastases (bone, liver, other visceral), as well as disease severity (minimal/extensive). However, there is no specific category or data provided for the "Mode of metastases" distinguishing between synchronous and metachronous presentation for either arm. The text on page 1 mentions "newly diagnosed metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer," which implies synchronous, but no numerical data is provided for this specific characteristic.

The median PFS in the orteronel (treatment) group was 47.6 months, as stated in the abstract on page 1 and the 'Secondary End Points' section on page 4. This is also visually represented in Figure 2 on page 4.
The median PFS in the control group was 23.0 months, as stated in the abstract on page 1 and the 'Secondary End Points' section on page 4. This is also visually represented in Figure 2 on page 4.
From Table A3 on page 13, under the 'Extensive' disease group, the 'PFS Orteronel Median/5 Year' is '2.2 years'. Converting to months: 2.2 * 12 = 26.4 months.
From Table A3 on page 13, under the 'Extensive' disease group, the 'PFS Control Median/5 Year' is '1.1 years'. Converting to months: 1.1 * 12 = 13.2 months.
From Table A3 on page 13, under the 'Minimal' disease group, the 'PFS Orteronel Median/5 Year' is 'NR' (Not Reached).
From Table A3 on page 13, under the 'Minimal' disease group, the 'PFS Control Median/5 Year' is '3.6 years'. Converting to months: 3.6 * 12 = 43.2 months.

The number of deaths for the treatment arm (LHRHa plus orteronel) is 251, found in the 'Deaths' row of the table within Figure 1 on page 3. This is confirmed by the text in the 'Primary End Point' section on page 4, which states '251 in the orteronel group'.

The number of deaths for the control arm (LHRHa plus bicalutamide) is 274, found in the 'Deaths' row of the table within Figure 1 on page 3. This is confirmed by the text in the 'Primary End Point' section on page 4, which states '274 in the control group'.

The 'PURPOSE' section on page 1 states: 'The primary objective was the comparison of overall survival (OS), targeting a 33% improvement in median survival.' The 'End Points' section on page 3 further confirms: 'The primary end point was OS, which was defined as the time from random assignment to the date of death from any cause.'
Extract the '0' row under 'Zubrod PS,ª No. (%)' for the 'Orteronel (n = 638)' column from Table 1 on page 5. The value is 416 (65).
Extract the '0' row under 'Zubrod PS,ª No. (%)' for the 'Bicalutamide (n = 641)' column from Table 1 on page 5. The value is 442 (69).

Sum the '1' and '2' rows under 'Zubrod PS,ª No. (%)' for the 'Orteronel (n = 638)' column from Table 1 on page 5. For PS 1: 197 (31); for PS 2: 21 (3). Total N = 197 + 21 = 218. Total % = 31 + 3 = 34. The value is 218 (34).

Sum the '1' and '2' rows under 'Zubrod PS,ª No. (%)' for the 'Bicalutamide (n = 641)' column from Table 1 on page 5. For PS 1: 175 (27); for PS 2: 21 (3). Total N = 175 + 21 = 196. Total % = 27 + 3 = 30. The value is 196 (30).

The PDF was reviewed for mentions of 'Quality of Life Scale', 'quality of life', 'QoL', or patient-reported outcomes as study endpoints or results. The 'PURPOSE' and 'METHODS' sections on page 1, 'End Points' and 'Assessments' on page 3, and 'RESULTS' and 'Secondary End Points' on page 4 describe the primary and secondary endpoints as overall survival (OS), progression-free survival (PFS), prostate-specific antigen (PSA) response, and adverse events (AEs). Table 2 on page 7 details adverse events. No specific 'Quality of Life Scale' or its results were reported in the document.

I searched the 'abstract' on page 1, the 'METHODS' section on page 2, and the 'End Points' and 'Assessments' sections on page 3 for mentions of 'Quality of Life', 'QoL', or 'patient-reported outcomes'. The primary and secondary endpoints are explicitly listed as overall survival (OS), progression-free survival (PFS), prostate-specific antigen (PSA) response rates, and adverse events (AEs) rate. Quality of Life was not reported as an endpoint or outcome in this study.

The secondary endpoints for the SWOG-1216 trial are progression-free survival (PFS), prostate-specific antigen (PSA) response rates, and adverse event (AE) profile. This information is explicitly stated in the abstract on page 1, and further detailed in the 'End Points' section on page 3 and the 'Secondary End Points' section on page 4.

The total number of participants randomly assigned is 1,313, as explicitly stated in the CONSORT diagram (Figure A1) on page 13, under 'Total randomly assigned (N = 1,313)'. This is corroborated by the 'RESULTS' section on page 4, which states '1,313 patients were randomly assigned'.

columns.column_name: Adverse Events - N (%) | Treatment-related Grade 5 | Treatment
columns.extraction_plan: The text on page 4 states 'Five patients in the experimental arm... had grade 5 AEs'. The footnote 'a' of Table 2 (page 7) further details these as 'myocardial infarction (two), stroke (one) and bronchiectasis (one) and is nonspecified (one) in the LHRHa plus orteronel arm', summing to 5. The note under Table 2 implies these are treatment-related or not unrelated. N=5 (0.8% of 627 patients).

columns.column_name: Adverse Events - N (%) | Treatment-related Grade 5 | Control
columns.extraction_plan: The text on page 4 states '...one patient in the control arm had grade 5 AEs'. The footnote 'a' of Table 2 (page 7) further details this as 'multiorgan failure (one) in the LHRHa plus bicalutamide arm'. The note under Table 2 implies these are treatment-related or not unrelated. N=1 (0.2% of 629 patients).

The document defines secondary endpoints on page 1 and page 3. These include progression-free survival (PFS) and prostate-specific antigen (PSA) level at 7 months, but not a standalone 'Time To PSA progression' (TTPSA) metric. PFS is a composite endpoint that includes PSA progression as one of its components, but TTPSA is not reported separately. I looked in the abstract (page 1), the 'End Points' section (page 3), and the 'Secondary End Points' section (page 4) and did not find this specific metric.

PFS is a composite endpoint that includes PSA progression as one of its components, but TTPSA is not reported separately. The 'METHODS' section on page 1 describes the experimental arm: 'ADT with orteronel (300 mg oral twice daily)'.

The number of patients in the treatment arm (ADT plus orteronel arm) is reported in the 'Results' section of the abstract on page 1 as 638. This is confirmed in the 'Patients' subsection of 'RESULTS' on page 4 and in Table 1 on page 5, where it states 'Orteronel (n = 638)'.

The 'Methods' section on page 1 states: 'patients with metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer were randomly assigned 1:1 to ADT with orteronel (300 mg oral twice daily; experimental arm) or ADT with bicalutamide (50 mg oral once daily; control arm).' Therefore, the treatment classes are 'ADT with orteronel' and 'ADT with bicalutamide'.

The experimental arm received ADT with orteronel (300 mg oral twice daily). The control arm received ADT with bicalutamide (50 mg oral once daily). This information is found in the 'METHODS' section of the abstract on page 1 and further detailed in the 'Patients and Interventions' section on page 2.

Table 1 on page 5, under 'Race, No. (%)', provides counts and percentages for 'White', 'Black', and 'Others'. These sum to 100% for the control arm, indicating no separate 'Unknown' category is reported.

The study involved two arms: an experimental arm receiving ADT with orteronel (300 mg oral twice daily) and a control arm receiving ADT with bicalutamide (50 mg oral once daily). This information is found in the abstract on page 1 and the 'Methods' section under 'Patients and Interventions' on page 2.

The trial is identified as "SWOG-1216" in the article title on page 1. The "Trial Design and Conduct" section on page 2 explicitly states, "The S1216 trial was a phase III, randomized, open-label, multicenter trial..." Additionally, page 8 lists the "CLINICAL TRIAL INFORMATION" as "NCT01809691 (SWOG S1216)".

columns.column_name: Reporting by prognostic groups - Y/N | Synchronous
columns.extraction_plan: The study population is described as patients with "newly diagnosed metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer" (Page 1, abstract; Page 2, methods), which implies a synchronous presentation of metastases. However, the document does not explicitly report or stratify outcomes by 'synchronous' versus 'metachronous' prognostic groups. I reviewed the Methods (Page 2), Results (Page 4), and tables (Table 1 on Page 5, Table A3 on Page 13) for such stratification but found none.

columns.column_name: Reporting by prognostic groups - Y/N | Metachronous
columns.extraction_plan: The study population is described as patients with "newly diagnosed metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer" (Page 1, abstract; Page 2, methods). The document does not explicitly report or stratify outcomes by 'metachronous' versus 'synchronous' prognostic groups. I reviewed the Methods (Page 2), Results (Page 4), and tables (Table 1 on Page 5, Table A3 on Page 13) for such stratification but found none.

columns.column_name: Reporting by prognostic groups - Y/N | High volume
columns.extraction_plan: Yes, reporting by 'high volume' prognostic group is available. The document uses the term 'Extensive' disease, which is explicitly defined as 'high risk' in footnote b of Table A2 on Page 12. Table A3 on Page 13, titled 'Treatment Outcomes by Disease Severity', provides outcomes (PFS and OS) for the 'Extensive' group. The definition of 'extensive disease' is also provided in the 'Patients and Interventions' section on Page 2: 'extensive disease was defined as that with greater than minimal involvement'.

columns.column_name: Reporting by prognostic groups - Y/N | Low volume
columns.extraction_plan: Yes, reporting by 'low volume' prognostic group is available. The document uses the term 'Minimal' disease, which is the counterpart to 'Extensive' (high volume/risk) disease. Table A3 on Page 13, titled 'Treatment Outcomes by Disease Severity', provides outcomes (PFS and OS) for the 'Minimal' group. The definition of 'minimal disease' is provided in the 'Patients and Interventions' section on Page 2: 'Minimal disease was defined as involvement of vertebrae and/or pelvic bones and/or lymph nodes'.
